name: The d- and f-Block Elements
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Position in Periodic Table
sections.body:
The d-block elements (transition metals) lie between s- and p-blocks, in groups 3–12. Their last electron enters a (n−1)d orbital, with general configuration (n−1)d1−10·ns1−2. There are four series:

  • 3d (1st series): Sc → Zn (Z = 21–30)
  • 4d (2nd series): Y → Cd
  • 5d (3rd series): La, Hf → Hg
  • 6d (4th series): Ac, Rf → Cn (incomplete)

A transition element is defined as one whose atom (or its common ion) has a partially filled d-subshell. By this definition Zn, Cd, Hg are not strictly transition metals (d¹⁰ in M and M²⁺), though included in the d-block for convenience.

f-block (inner transition): lanthanoids (4f, Ce → Lu) and actinoids (5f, Th → Lr).

sections.heading: Electronic Configurations and Anomalies
sections.body:
Two well-known irregularities in the 3d series due to extra stability of half-filled and fully-filled d subshells:

  • Cr: [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ (not 3d⁴ 4s²) — half-filled d⁵ stable.
  • Cu: [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ (not 3d⁹ 4s²) — fully-filled d¹⁰ stable.

For the cations of transition metals, electrons are removed from 4s before 3d. So Fe = [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s², but Fe²⁺ = [Ar] 3d⁶ and Fe³⁺ = [Ar] 3d⁵.

sections.heading: General Properties of Transition Metals
sections.body:
Atomic and ionic radii: decrease across a series due to increasing nuclear charge, but only slowly because added d-electrons screen poorly. From 4d to 5d series, radii hardly increase due to lanthanoid contraction (filling of 4f in lanthanoids before 5d series). Hence Zr ≈ Hf, Nb ≈ Ta in size and chemistry.

Density and m.p./b.p.: high (strong metallic bonding from d-electrons participating). Maxima around the middle of each series (Cr, Mo, W).

Ionisation enthalpy: gradually increases across a series; broadly higher than s-block. Irregularities at d⁵ and d¹⁰ configurations.

Metallic character: all are metals, hard, ductile, good conductors of heat and electricity.

Standard electrode potentials: E°(M²⁺/M) becomes less negative across the series; Cu has positive E° (+0.34 V), explaining why it does not displace H2 from acids.

sections.heading: Variable Oxidation States
sections.body:
Transition metals show multiple oxidation states because (n−1)d and ns electrons are close in energy. The most common oxidation states in the 3d series are:

  • Sc: +3 only
  • Ti: +2, +3, +4
  • V: +2 to +5
  • Cr: +2 to +6 (most stable +3)
  • Mn: +2 to +7 (most stable +2)
  • Fe: +2, +3 (stable +3 = d⁵)
  • Co, Ni: +2, +3
  • Cu: +1, +2
  • Zn: +2 only

Highest oxidation states (Mn(VII), Cr(VI)) appear with O²⁻ or F⁻ as ligands. Sc (no d electrons in +3) and Zn (full d¹⁰ in +2) show only one oxidation state.

sections.heading: Coloured Ions, Magnetic Properties, Catalytic Activity
sections.body:
Colour: most transition-metal ions are coloured because partially-filled d orbitals allow d–d transitions by absorbing visible light; the complementary colour is transmitted. Sc³⁺ (d⁰), Ti⁴⁺ (d⁰), Cu⁺ (d¹⁰), Zn²⁺ (d¹⁰) are colourless.

Magnetic moment: from spin-only formula μ = √(n(n+2)) BM, where n is the number of unpaired electrons. (Bohr magneton, BM = 9.27 × 10⁻²⁴ J T⁻¹.) Ions with unpaired electrons are paramagnetic; with all paired, diamagnetic.

Catalytic activity: due to (i) variable oxidation states and (ii) ability to form intermediate complexes with reactants. Examples: V2O5 (contact process), Fe (Haber), Ni (catalytic hydrogenation), Pd/Pt (many).

Interstitial compounds: small atoms (H, C, N, B) occupy interstitial sites in metal lattices, giving very hard, high-melting, chemically inert solids (e.g. steel, TiC, Mn4N).

Alloys: form readily because of similar atomic radii (e.g. brass = Cu + Zn, bronze = Cu + Sn, steel = Fe + C + others).

sections.heading: Important Compounds — KMnO₄ and K₂Cr₂O₇
sections.body:
Potassium permanganate KMnO₄: prepared by fusion of MnO2 with KOH and an oxidising agent (KNO3) to give K2MnO4 (green), then electrolytic or chemical oxidation in alkaline solution. It is a powerful oxidising agent in acidic, neutral, and alkaline media:

  • Acidic: MnO4⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H2O (E° = +1.51 V).
  • Neutral/Slightly alkaline: MnO4⁻ + 2H2O + 3e⁻ → MnO2 + 4OH⁻.
  • Strongly alkaline: MnO4⁻ + e⁻ → MnO4²⁻.

Used in titrations (oxalate, Fe²⁺), bleaching, water treatment, and as a disinfectant.

Potassium dichromate K₂Cr₂O₇: from chromite ore FeCr2O4: fused with Na2CO3 and air → Na2CrO4, acidified to Na2Cr2O7, treated with KCl. Orange-red crystals.

Equilibrium: 2 CrO4²⁻ (yellow) + 2H⁺ ⇌ Cr2O7²⁻ (orange) + H2O. Strong oxidiser only in acidic medium: Cr2O7²⁻ + 14H⁺ + 6e⁻ → 2Cr³⁺ + 7H2O (E° = +1.33 V). Used in volumetric analysis and leather tanning.

sections.heading: Lanthanoids — General Trends
sections.body:
Lanthanoids: 14 elements Ce (Z=58) to Lu (Z=71); general configuration [Xe] 4f1−14 5d0−1 6s². Common oxidation state +3; +2 (Eu, Yb) and +4 (Ce, Tb) occasionally seen.

Lanthanoid contraction: steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii (Ln³⁺) across the series due to poor shielding by 4f electrons. Consequences:

  • Similar size of 4d and 5d series (Zr ≈ Hf, Nb ≈ Ta).
  • Similar chemistry of all lanthanoids → difficult separation.
  • Slight increase in basicity from Ce(OH)3 to Lu(OH)3... actually basicity decreases from La(OH)3 to Lu(OH)3 as size decreases.

All lanthanoids are silvery-white, highly reactive metals (less than alkaline earths). Used in misch metal (Ce + La + others) for lighter flints, and in alloys.

sections.heading: Actinoids — General Features
sections.body:
Actinoids: 14 elements Th (Z=90) to Lr (Z=103); general configuration [Rn] 5f1−14 6d0−1 7s². All are radioactive; only Th and U are found in significant amounts in nature.

Show variable oxidation states (+3 to +7) because 5f, 6d, 7s have similar energies. Most common is +3.

Actinoid contraction: similar to lanthanoid contraction but more pronounced because 5f electrons screen even more poorly than 4f.

Comparison with lanthanoids: actinoids show greater range of oxidation states; their compounds are more easily hydrolysed; they are more reactive; coloured ions are due to f–f transitions.

summary: The d-block (transition metals) and f-block (inner-transition: lanthanoids and actinoids) are characterised by partially-filled (n−1)d and (n−2)f orbitals respectively. Distinctive features include variable oxidation states, coloured ions from d–d transitions, paramagnetism (μ = √(n(n+2)) BM), catalytic activity, complex formation, and alloy/interstitial-compound formation. Anomalous configurations of Cr (3d⁵4s¹) and Cu (3d¹⁰4s¹) reflect half/full d-shell stability. Lanthanoid contraction makes 4d ≈ 5d radii. KMnO₄ and K₂Cr₂O₇ are key oxidising agents (E° = +1.51 V and +1.33 V in acid). Actinoids resemble lanthanoids but are radioactive with a wider range of oxidation states.
pitfalls: Calling Zn, Cd, Hg "transition metals" — strictly they are not (d¹⁰ in common ion).
Removing 3d electrons before 4s when forming cations — wrong; 4s leaves first.
Using μ = √(n(n+2)) for orbital + spin contribution — formula is spin-only.
Forgetting that K₂Cr₂O₇ is a strong oxidiser only in acidic medium; KMnO₄ works in all.
Confusing the lanthanoid contraction (4f) with actinoid contraction (5f).
Ignoring the fact that Mn shows +7 (highest in 3d series) due to 3d⁵ 4s² electrons available.
